skill.name: code-review
skill.description: Reviews changed files for security vulnerabilities, performance problems, logic bugs, and project standard violations using a structured code-review workflow.
skill.summary: Structured code review for changed files, with OWASP, ORM, performance, and project standards checks.
skill.author: DavidDworetzky

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical patterns, but the dangerous code snippets are reference examples for code reviewers rather than executable skill logic. Confirmed risk is limited to local git command execution and repository file access, with no evidence of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious network behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local Git Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to use git diff commands and includes a shell helper that runs git commands to identify changed files. This is legitimate for a code-review workflow, but it should be disclosed because it executes local commands in the user repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and are central to the skill workflow. Confidence is high that command execution occurs, but the commands are limited to git diff operations and show no malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Project Standards Paths
low_findings.description: The skill references absolute local paths for Geist project standards. This is not malicious, but it is non-portable and may cause assistants to read files outside the current repository if those paths exist.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The absolute paths are directly present in the instructions. The risk is limited because they point to project documentation and there is no network transmission or secret handling.

low_findings.title: Static Dangerous-Code Findings Are Reference Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged eval, exec, pickle, subprocess, os.system, innerHTML, API key, database password, HTTP, and weak-crypto patterns in OWASP reference material. These are examples of vulnerable and secure code for reviewers to recognize, not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context labels these snippets as vulnerable or secure examples in a quick reference. I found no evidence that the skill executes these snippets or uses them to access credentials.

low_findings.title: Static ORM Backtick Findings Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Ruby or shell backtick patterns in the ORM reference. The referenced file is Markdown documentation with inline code names and fenced examples, not Ruby or shell execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is an ORM optimization reference and the flagged areas are explanatory Python or Markdown examples. No executable Ruby backtick invocation or shell command construction is present in that file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Illustrative Dangerous Patterns in Security Reference
dangerous_patterns.description: The OWASP reference intentionally includes dangerous examples such as shell command construction, dynamic evaluation, unsafe deserialization, direct DOM insertion, hardcoded secrets, and insecure transport. These patterns are documentation for review guidance and should not be treated as executable malicious behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples appear under named OWASP review sections and include vulnerable and secure labels. This strongly supports a benign educational purpose despite matching dangerous code patterns.

content.user_title: Review Changed Code for Bugs and Security
content.value_statement: Manual reviews can miss security, performance, and logic issues in changed files. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a structured review of git diffs and project standards.
actual_capabilities: Identifies changed files using git diff commands or the included helper script.
Reviews code against OWASP Top 10 security categories.
Checks SQLAlchemy and ORM usage for N+1 queries, eager loading, indexing, and counting issues.
Looks for logic bugs, edge cases, error handling gaps, and type-safety problems.
Compares changed code with project standards from local guidance files when available.
Separates urgent findings from recommended improvements in the review output.
limitations: It depends on local repository context and cannot review code that is not available to the assistant.
It provides guidance and examples, but it does not replace dedicated security testing or dependency scanning.
Project-standard checks reference Geist-specific files and may need adaptation for other repositories.
The helper script only lists changed files; the review quality depends on the assistant reading the relevant code.

use_cases.title: Pre-commit review
use_cases.description: Review local changes before committing to catch security, logic, and performance issues early.

use_cases.title: Pull request hardening
use_cases.description: Check a focused diff for OWASP risks, ORM problems, and risky implementation choices before review.

use_cases.title: ORM performance audit
use_cases.description: Inspect SQLAlchemy changes for N+1 queries, inefficient counting, missing indexes, and large result loading.

prompt_templates.title: Review my current changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my changed files for bugs, security issues, and performance problems. Start with the git diff and list findings by severity.

prompt_templates.title: Check staged files only
prompt_templates.prompt: Review only my staged files. Prioritize correctness and OWASP Top 10 security issues, then list recommended improvements separately.

prompt_templates.title: Audit ORM changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the changed SQLAlchemy code for N+1 queries, inefficient counting, missing eager loading, and missing indexes.

prompt_templates.title: Review recent commit range
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the files changed in the last three commits. Use project standards where available and include exact file and line references for each finding.

output_examples.input: Review my unstaged backend changes before I commit.
output: Critical: No confirmed critical issues found.
Important: One endpoint lacks an authorization check before returning user-specific data.
Recommended: Add eager loading to avoid repeated relationship queries in the user list path.

output_examples.input: Check this SQLAlchemy diff for performance issues.
output: Finding: A relationship is accessed inside a loop without eager loading.
Impact: The query count grows with the number of rows returned.
Fix: Load the relationship with selectinload or restructure the query as a batch operation.

output_examples.input: Review the staged files for security only.
output: High: User input reaches a shell command without argument separation.
Medium: Sensitive error details may be returned to clients.
No evidence found for insecure deserialization in the reviewed diff.

best_practices: Run the review from the repository root so git commands and local standards resolve correctly.
Ask for findings by severity and require exact file and line references for each issue.
Confirm suggested fixes with tests or targeted checks before merging.
anti_patterns: Do not treat reference examples as findings in the reviewed project without matching evidence in changed files.
Do not apply broad refactors while reviewing a narrow diff unless the user asks for them.
Do not skip authorization, input validation, or ORM checks because a change appears small.

faq.question: Does this skill run a full security scanner?
faq.answer: No. It guides an assistant through manual review patterns and local git diffs.

faq.question: Can it review unstaged and staged changes?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow includes git commands for all changes, staged changes, unstaged changes, and recent commits.

faq.question: Does it require SQLAlchemy?
faq.answer: No. General review guidance still applies, but the ORM reference is focused on SQLAlchemy.

faq.question: Will it automatically fix issues?
faq.answer: The skill suggests fixing urgent issues, but users should confirm changes and run tests before merging.

faq.question: Is it suitable for Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: What project context improves results?
faq.answer: Relevant coding standards, test commands, changed files, and expected behavior make the review more accurate.
